question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: negotiate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that negotiate() sets VARY_HEADER to 'accept-encoding' on every cache hit response, causing downstream proxies to cache separate variants per Accept-Encoding value, and then poison one variant (e.g., gzip) with a manipulated transaction history while the identity variant remains correct, reorganizing transaction history for gzip-capable clients?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: shouldCompress()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a request that returns a transaction history response body just below the config.cache.response.compressThreshold (150 bytes by default) to prevent compression, then exploit the uncompressed storage path to observe timing differences that reveal whether specific transactions exist in the history, allowing inference about transaction ordering without direct access?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the logger.info() call that logs req.ip, req.method, req.originalUrl in responseCacheCheckHandler() by crafting a URL with log-injection characters (e.g., newlines, ANSI codes) to inject false log entries that misrepresent transaction history access patterns, obscuring a cache poisoning attack from defenders?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of request body inclusion in cacheKeyGenerator() (only req.originalUrl is hashed) to cause POST-like semantics (if any endpoint accepts POST for transaction queries) to share cache keys with GET requests, serving a GET-cached transaction history in response to a POST query with different filter parameters?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the static CACHE_KEY_VERSION_SUFFIX = '-v1' in cacheKeyGenerator() to predict the exact Redis key format (MD5(url) + '-v1') and, if they gain any write access to Redis (e.g., via SSRF), overwrite a transaction history cache entry with a crafted response that reorders transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the singleton cache instance (getCache() lazy initialization) by sending requests during the Redis reconnection window (when this.ready = false in Cache) to bypass the cache entirely, causing all users to receive fresh DB queries that may return inconsistent transaction orderings during a high-write period?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the cache serves responses with the remaining Redis TTL as the max-age (public, max-age=${redisTtl}) to cause a client to cache a transaction history response locally for a very short TTL (e.g., 1 second), then immediately re-request and receive a different ordering from the DB, creating an inconsistent view of transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the condition (isUnmodified || httpStatusCodes.isSuccess(res.statusCode)) in responseCacheUpdateHandler() to cause a 206 Partial Content response (which isSuccess() considers successful) to be cached, serving a partial/truncated transaction history to subsequent users who expect a full 200 OK response?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a 206 Partial Content response for a transaction history endpoint and cause it to be cached by responseCacheUpdateHandler() (since httpStatusCodes.isSuccess() returns true for 206), so that subsequent users querying the same URL receive only a partial transaction list, effectively hiding transactions from the history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of authentication context in cacheKeyGenerator() (only URL is hashed) to cause a transaction history response for account A to be cached under a key that is also valid for account B's query (if both queries produce the same URL), serving account A's transaction history to account B and reorganizing their apparent transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a Host header or tenant identifier in cacheKeyGenerator() to cause transaction history responses cached for one virtual host to be served to users of a different virtual host (in a multi-tenant deployment), reorganizing the transaction history visible to users of the second host?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that responseCacheCheckHandler() does not validate the integrity of the cached response body (no HMAC or signature check on redisValue) to cause a corrupted or attacker-modified Redis entry (via SSRF or Redis misconfiguration) to be served as a legitimate transaction history, reorganizing the apparent order of transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Object.assign(new CachedApiResponse(), redisValue) pattern in responseCacheCheckHandler() to perform prototype pollution by injecting a __proto__ key into the Redis-stored JSON, overriding CachedApiResponse methods like getBody() or getUncompressedBody() to return a manipulated transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that cachedResponse.headers is spread directly into the response headers ({...cachedResponse.headers, ...}) without sanitization, allowing a crafted Redis entry to inject arbitrary response headers (e.g., X-Transaction-Order: manipulated) that mislead clients about the ordering of transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the ETag comparison (conditionalHeader === cachedResponse.headers[ETAG_HEADER]) being a strict string equality check to send a crafted if-none-match header with a wildcard (*) value that does not match the ETag, forcing a full response delivery of a stale cached transaction history instead of triggering a fresh DB query?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the deletion of CONTENT_LENGTH_HEADER before caching (delete headers[CONTENT_LENGTH_HEADER]) in responseCacheUpdateHandler() combined with negotiate() re-computing content-length via getLength() (which uses an approximation: Math.floor((body.length * 3) / 4)) to cause an incorrect content-length to be sent to clients, causing HTTP clients to truncate the transaction history response body?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: negotiate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the getLength() approximation in CachedApiResponse (Math.floor((body.length * 3) / 4)) used by negotiate() to set an incorrect content-length header for gzip-encoded responses, causing HTTP/1.1 clients that rely on content-length to truncate the transaction history body and miss the last few transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that cacheKeyGenerator() uses req.originalUrl (which includes the full path and query string) without normalizing whitespace or special characters, by sending a URL with encoded spaces (%20) in query parameter values to generate a unique cache key that bypasses the existing cache and forces a fresh DB query with potentially different transaction ordering?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the cache-control header being set to 'public' (public, max-age=${redisTtl}) in responseCacheCheckHandler() to cause shared/public CDN caches to store and serve the transaction history response to all users, allowing an attacker who primes the CDN cache with a specific transaction ordering to reorganize the history visible to all CDN users?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that responseCacheUpdateHandler() caches the response body from res.locals[responseBodyLabel] without validating its content against the original DB query result, allowing a middleware injection (if any middleware modifies responseBodyLabel) to cache a manipulated transaction history body?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests with different timestamp filter parameters (e.g., timestamp=gte:X vs timestamp=gt:X-1) that are semantically equivalent but produce different MD5 hashes in cacheKeyGenerator(), causing the cache to store separate entries with potentially different transaction orderings due to boundary condition differences in the DB query?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the timing window between a cache miss (responseCacheCheckHandler returns without serving) and the cache update (responseCacheUpdateHandler stores the result) by sending a flood of concurrent requests to the same transaction history URL, causing a cache stampede where multiple DB queries with different result sets race to be stored, resulting in non-deterministic transaction ordering being cached?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheCheckHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the cache key does not include the X-Forwarded-For or X-Real-IP headers to cause transaction history responses cached for one IP address to be served to users from different IP addresses, potentially serving a response that was generated with IP-based filtering applied to a user for whom that filtering should not apply?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: responseCacheUpdateHandler()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the condition that responseCacheUpdateHandler() only caches when responseBody is truthy and responseCacheKey is set, by crafting a request that causes the response body to be an empty string (falsy), preventing caching and forcing every subsequent user to receive a fresh DB query that may return a different transaction ordering?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/responseCacheHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheKeyGenerator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of query parameter value validation in cacheKeyGenerator() by injecting null bytes (%00) into query parameter values to generate a unique MD5 hash that bypasses the existing cache, forcing a fresh DB query that may return a different transaction ordering than what is currently cached?
